note_data.title: Static electricity
keyPoints: **Static electricity** is caused by the transfer of electrons from one material to another through friction
Objects become **positively charged** when they lose electrons and **negatively charged** when they gain electrons
**Like charges repel** and **unlike charges attract** each other
An **electric field** is a region where a charged object experiences a force
**Electric field lines** run from positive to negative charges and show the direction of force on a positive charge
Objects can be charged by **friction**, **contact** (conduction), or **induction**
**Earthing** (grounding) removes static charge by allowing electrons to flow to or from the ground
**Sparks** occur when charge builds up to a point where air becomes conducting
**Gold leaf electroscope** detects the presence and type of electric charge

definitions.definition: Electric charge that builds up on objects and does not flow
A region around a charged object where another charged object experiences a force
The process of removing excess charge by connecting an object to the ground
A material that does not allow electric charge to flow through it easily
A material that allows electric charge to flow through it easily

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of steps or detailed account of what happens
Always label electric field lines with arrows showing direction

modelAnswer.question: A plastic rod is rubbed with a cloth. The rod becomes negatively charged. Explain how the rod becomes charged and state what happens to the cloth.
modelAnswer.answer: Friction between the rod and cloth causes electrons to transfer from the cloth to the rod. The rod gains electrons so becomes negatively charged. The cloth loses electrons so becomes positively charged. Charge is conserved in this process.

commonMistakes: Never say 'charge flows' in insulators — always say 'electrons transfer'
Students often say objects gain or lose 'charge' instead of 'electrons'
Never say 'positive charges move' — only electrons move in most materials
Students confuse charging by induction with charging by contact
Drawing electric field lines without arrows or in wrong direction loses marks
examinerTips: Static electricity appears almost every year in Paper 2
Always specify 'electrons' rather than just 'charge' when explaining charging
Electric field diagrams must show arrows pointing from + to - for full marks
Questions often ask about dangers and uses of static electricity
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Electric field pattern around point charges showing field lines as arrows pointing from positive to negative charges, with lines closer together indicating stronger field
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how a photocopier uses static electricity and describe the charging process
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (3 marks) — students had to identify methods of charging objects and explain charge distribution
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — candidates needed to explain lightning formation and suggest safety precautions during thunderstorms
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — students had to explain why a charged comb attracts small pieces of paper

workedExample.question: A plastic rod is rubbed with a cloth. The rod becomes negatively charged. (a) Explain how the rod becomes negatively charged. (2 marks) (b) The charged rod is brought near small pieces of paper. Explain why the paper is attracted to the rod. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that friction causes electron transfer between materials
Step 2: Explain that electrons move from cloth to rod, making rod negatively charged
Step 3: Describe induced charges - rod causes electrons in paper to be repelled to far side
Step 4: Explain that near side of paper becomes positively charged
Step 5: State that opposite charges attract, so paper moves toward rod
workedExample.answer: (a) When the plastic rod is rubbed with cloth, friction causes electrons to transfer from the cloth to the rod. The rod gains extra electrons and becomes negatively charged. (b) The negatively charged rod repels electrons in the paper to the far side. This leaves the near side of the paper positively charged. Since opposite charges attract, the paper is attracted to the negatively charged rod.
workedExample.markScheme: Part (a): 1 mark for mentioning electron transfer/friction, 1 mark for explaining rod gains electrons. Part (b): 1 mark for induced charges, 1 mark for explaining charge separation in paper, 1 mark for attraction of opposite charges

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused current electricity with static electricity' — always distinguish between moving charges (current) and stationary charges (static) in your answers

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently wrote that charges are created during rubbing' — remember that charges are transferred, not created. State clearly that electrons move from one material to another

Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often failed to explain induction properly' — when describing attraction to neutral objects, always mention that charges are induced (separated) in the neutral object, creating opposite charges on near and far surfaces
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw charge distribution diagrams showing: (1) A charged object (rod/balloon) with + or - symbols evenly distributed on its surface, (2) A neutral object nearby with induced charges - opposite charges on the near side, same charges on the far side, (3) Clear labels showing 'negatively charged rod', 'induced positive charges', 'induced negative charges', (4) An arrow indicating direction of attraction between opposite charges. Include proper spacing and use + and - symbols consistently.
note_data.quickRecap: Static electricity involves stationary electric charges created by friction transferring electrons between materials. Objects with same charges repel, opposite charges attract. Charged objects can attract neutral objects by inducing charge separation. Lightning occurs when static charge builds up in clouds and discharges to earth through the air.
